This PR should regenerate the lockfiles imo, as if we had the consumption option set to `error`. Given that the format is changing. 

All call sites will also need to be updated to no longer do that `await Get(PythonLockfileRequest, ToolSentinel)` - that's the whole motivation for this change, that we don't need to evaluate the global ICs for that tool anymore, only the context's ICs. That can be in a followup, though. I recommend splitting this up into 3 PRs:

1. Add the `InterpreterConstraints.is_compatible()` method.
2. Land this PR, which changes the data format to JSON and treats ICs as a separate field.
3. Leverage this PR to update call sites so that they're not wasting work as much as they do now.

A few remarks:

* Paste text instead of screenshots. It makes it easier to grab the video ID for others to test, for example:
```python
from pytube import YouTube

yt = YouTube("https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xIANCyXGN5I")

for k,v in enumerate(yt.streams):
    try:
        print(f"{k}, fps: {v.fps}")    
    except Exception as e:
        print(e)
        print(f"{k}, {v}")

for stream in yt.streams.filter(fps=30):
    print(f"filtered: {stream}")
    assert hasattr(stream, "fps")
```
* It might be a good idea to add some tests in test_query.py

Other than that, after testing a bit, it looks good to me. Good patch!

(I haven't looked at feedback from others - so some may have already been covered by others - but i wanted to call them out so that we know which ones we feel the same about)

Things i like:
- Module level import is neat. I feel it could address the confusion around "when should i put things in __init__ and when not".
- Resources at the top-level. I found myself wanting top-level resource a lot more often as I'm writing analytical pipelines that share resources, esp when we are using a db as the single source of truth I feel top-level resources can save a lot of typing and make collab easier because configs can be shared by default.
- Typed API is amazing.

Things i have concerns about:
- Production education concerns. Would like to get  @slopp's to weight in.
  * Calling `definitions(..)` in `__init__.py` will need education. For example, just by looking at the code, I'm not sure when the definitions will be loaded.
  * Although I personally like the module-level function call, it may cause onboarding issue for users who are not very familiar with python, as in they may not understand the ` __init__.py` files and ignore it entirely.

Questions
- How would one do `RepositoryDefinition.load_asset_value` in the new world?
- v1.1 or any minor versions (by minor i mean 1.x) feels a right time to introduce changes like this. should we get this in v1.1 so we can get feedback earlier?
- Similar question^ how do we plan to roll this out? Although it's a non-invasive change, it'd involve a lot of docs/examples changes, which I'd also consider as breaking changes to content.

```     CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE {destination_table} AS (```

(i.e., you would revert `build_query()`'s function signature to the existing version, and add `destination_table='{destination_table}'` to the `format()` call.

Then in `BigQueryContext.run_script_or_fetch()`, we would call 
`self.client.query(sql.format(destination_table=fqtn)).result()`... and possibly be able to replace `BigQueryContext.run_query()` with this new version as a drop-in replacement?

Might be good not to introduce the inconsistency of `results_table` vs `destination_table`. Unless you really hate `results_table`, I suggest it should win since it's there already; if you do hate it then go change everything over to `destination_table`.

Thanks for pushing this up Ryan.  It looks really promising and I think it would be a popular feature.

My understanding is that you want to modify the implementation to enable import / export of nested data structures.  So I could choose to export a Book with Authors, Categories etc.  Then I could theoretically import this into some other environment.

It seems sensible to take a bit of time on this, so I think that this would be suitable for release 4 rather than release 3.  I'm happy to help out if my schedule permits.

I agree that you should stick to JSON only to start with to keep things simple.  I guess most people would choose this format for nested representations.

I had a look at the draft PR.  I'm not keen on the idea of the widget class doing the heavy lifting with imports.  I think it creates significant dependencies between widgets / resources and the import process, and I think it would be better to keep these separate.

My first thought is that nested representations have been implemented effectively in DRF using Serializers.  So I wonder if this could be a useful place to start for the design.  Obviously it would be easy for users to understand this if they are familiar with DRF, and if we plug-in their implementation then we benefit from the stability of their Serializer implementation.

I expect using DRF serializers would have to be an optional plugin in order to maintain backwards compatibility.  This might involve a significant re-write of our existing code.  I have not thought about it in much depth yet.

So I think this would be a great feature, but it looks like it could be a sizeable chunk of work.  Is there anything else out there that imports / exports nested data?

Apologies if I've not understood in full detail.  I'd be happy to get on a call if you wanted to discuss it further.

What are your thoughts?

These are great improvements, but they don't really change the story with FairScale.

One thing that's missing is synchronization across distributed workers when gathering model and training state, since collecting the state associated with sharded parameters requires a distributed `gather` operation (each worker needs to send its shard of the data to the main process).

Another issue is that the optimizer state actually has to be collected through the `FullyShardedDataParallel` model wrapper ([`gather_full_optim_state`](https://fairscale.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/nn/fsdp.html)).

May I kindly ask, what was the original idea of the PR? It seems that this PR tries to solve too many problems in one piece. It's really hard to review for me and to make the changes here for you.

I feel that the PR covers the following distinct topics:

1. Align how we use `axis` argument in low-level dataframe
2. Introduce new logic for working with `dtypes/copy_dtypes` parameters
3. Combine `map` and `broadcast_apply` into `map_partitions`
4. Combine `apply_full_axis` and `broadcast_apply_full_axis` into `map_partition_full_axis`
5. Combine `apply_select_indices` and `broadcast_apply_select_indices` into `map_select_indices`
6. Rework `apply_func_to_indices_both_axis` into `map_select_indices_both_axes`

All of these may be solved with small different PRs (rather than one huge). They're probably un-doable in parallel as some of them may block each other, however, I think the changes would make much more sense when introduced by small iterations.

Thanks for taking the time to contribute to LightGBM!

But this type of PR that touches this much code for only style reasons should be left to maintainers. While investigating issues, we sometimes use the [git blame](https://docs.github.com/en/github/managing-files-in-a-repository/tracking-changes-in-a-file) to see who most recently touched some piece of code, and then sometimes `@` that person for a second opinion. Having a large PR like this that touches many lines for only style reasons would make that more difficult.

We use `pycodestyle` and `pydocstyle` in this project to provide some minimal linting on Python code (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/fd094d2719a04b984b376a665b6c3e5e1aff2967/.ci/test.sh#L60-L61), but have avoided adopting `isort` or `black` so far. If you believe this project would benefit from such changes you are welcome to [open an issue](https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues) describing why, but I believe it should be a maintainer like @StrikerRUS who applies such changes.

If you're looking for ways to contribute, we'd welcome your help on some of the open issues. The "good first issues" list is a good place to start: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+label%3A%22good+first+issue%22.

Thanks very much for writing this up!

I'm convinced that `{lightgbm}` should produce a `(num_observations, num_classes)` matrix for multi-class classification, based on your examples of `{randomForest}` and `{ranger}`.

Instead of silently changing this behavior by altering a default, I think for the next release `{lightgbm}` should just remove the `reshape` argument from `predict.lgb.Booster()` and `Predictor$predict()` entirely. I cannot think of a reason that getting a vector would be preferable to getting a matrix like this:

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/60e72d5f4ec5ec2a4d30a53633e1e2d59c81309b/R-package/demo/multiclass.R#L59-L63

Removing `reshape` entirely would:

* make `{lightgbm}`'s behavior more consistent with other statistical packages in R (including R's standard library)
* break existing user code in a loud way with error that makes it clear what has change
* alleviate the inconsistency you've pointed out of the vector produced by `predict()` being in row-major order when R assumes column-major order
* simplify the codebase

Before making any changes like that, let's get some opinions from others.

@Laurae2 @mayer79 @StrikerRUS @shiyu1994 @guolinke what do you think about the following proposed breaking changes for the R package?

* remove argument `reshape` in `lgb.predict.Booster()` and `Predictor$predict()`
* if `reshape` is found in `...` for `lgb.predict.Booster()`, raise an informative error explaining that that parameter is no longer supported
* for predictions like those from multi-class classification, where more than one number is produced per row in the input data, have `lgb.predict.Booster()` and `Predictor$predict()` return a numeric matrix of shape `(num_observations, num_classes)`

A bit more involved case analysis but looking correct to me.

Logging some very helpful out-of-band comments from Ian here:

```
We look at the external schema (the one that generates the package for Foo) and run the generator pre-processor which determines what types are generated.
```

This is good. So we are improving on not generating dangling references. 

However backwards-compatibiltiy remains a bit fragile, there are unexpected constraints. To cite Ian's example:

>  Adding a new resource that references an old type in an optional way is breaking if if the type was not previously referenced in an optional way.  That is confusing, so here is a concrete example:
Given a schema:

```
resources:
  fooConfigurator:
    inputProperties:
      input:
        $ref: #/types/foo
        required: true
types:
  foo:
    prop1:
      type: string
    prop2:
      type: string
Adding the new resource:
resources:
  takesOptionalFoo:
    input:
      $ref: #/types/foo
```

> is a breaking change to downstream packages.
If there is a downstream package fooConsumer with schema:

```
resources:
  fooConsumer:
    inputProperties:
      input:
        $ref: foo#/types/foo
Before the schema of foo is updated, fooConsumer will generate the following go
type FooConsumerArgs struct {
  Input *foo.FooInput
}
but after the change (and consuming the new schema) fooConsumer will generate
type FooConsumerArgs struct {
  Input foo.FooPtrInput
}
This changes the public interface of fooConsumer in a breaking way.
```

We do not have a solution to the fundamental problem here, namely that we do global schema analysis to try to avoid generating unused helper types, but we miss that downstream packages may decide to use them; and therefore global changes to the schema may result in breaking changes in the downstream packages. But the PR is no worse than before so we think we should take it.

This looks good, but I left a few comments to be addressed before this goes in.

For my understanding, in the flow from the network through Plasma to building the DataFrame, where was the large memory inflation happening? My understanding of the flow is:
1. `ray.get()`: Plasma buffers are allocated and filled as objects are pulled, with the Arrow tables being deserialized directly into the Plasma buffers.
2. `builder.add()`: The block builder holds pointers to those Plasma buffers, so no new copies are made while add blocks to the builder.
3. `.build().to_pandas()`: The `.build()` and `.to_pandas()` calls create a copy or two during the Arrow table concatenation and the DataFrame construction, respectively.

This PR deals with stages (1) and (2) rather than the copy-heavy stage (3), and AFAICT wouldn't result in a lower peak during those two stages. Am I missing the creation of an extra copy or two in (1) and (2), maybe receiver-side queueing of byte chunks during pulls, or something else?

https://docs.dagster.io/community/contributing includes directions for getting your environment set up for testing. But because this is a narrowly scoped change, you might find that setup to be a bit heavy handed. Instead, I think the following should be sufficient to run the relevant tests:

```
pip install 'tox==3.25.0'
python_modules/libraries/dagster-aws/
tox -- -k ecs_tests
```

These tests run against a local mock of ECS. That mock uses [botocore's stubber](https://botocore.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/stubber.html) to ensure that calls to the actual ECS API are valid.

Historically, when we've extended our ECS code in similar ways ([like when we added volumes and mount points](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/13409/files#diff-83428dfde55d54583a7e6470d4f59378558e53d2ddce1143f98d02f7f7fd0b67)), we've also extended the existing tests to pass in the new parameter and assert that it gets set as expected.

We don't run any integration tests on this particular code but I'd recommend once you feel like your changes are sufficient, you run it on one of your own deployments and confirm that you can pull from your third-party repository.

Thanks a lot. On top of my inline comments, we can discuss about the testing part.

Arguably it is difficult to design the usual integrations tests, given that `vcrpy` is designed to mock HTTP over TCP calls, not other diagrams over TCP or UDP. For sure we could find another testing library to mock these calls. However at the beginning the HTTP mock approach is here to avoid to make actual calls against real production systems.

For DDNS in fact, we can go to a very different testing approach which would be:
1) Spin up a local RFC2136 compatible DNS server
2) Execute all tests for the `ddns` provider against this instance
3) Tear down the DNS server

Actually @alexzorin and I created this approach for the `rfc2136` DNS plugin in the Certbot project, using Docker (which is available in Azure Pipelines, responsible to execute tests on PRs). The relevant files are the following:
* Python logic to spin up the DNS server (bind9): https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-ci/certbot_integration_tests/utils/dns_server.py
* assets to configure the bind9 instance: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/master/certbot-ci/certbot_integration_tests/assets/bind-config

If you are willing to do the work in your PR to setup this testing approach, that would be great!

However I totally understand if you do not have the time, since it is not a negligeable work. Moreover due to the nature of these tests, it is totally possible to let someone else (most likely me) make the actual implementation later. In this case I would just ask you to create the test module in `lexicon.tests.providers` with an overall `skip` directive explaining that the tests need to be written in the future.
